headline: Chips and Energy Surge to Quarter-End as Breadth Holds Positive
body_html:
Equity markets are navigating a broadly constructive mid-session on the final trading day of the second quarter, with 114 of 198 names in our universe trading higher and the dominant theme unmistakably centered on semiconductor strength. The sector is advancing more than five percent intraday, pulling the broader tape along with it as investors position for what is shaping up to be Wall Street's strongest quarterly performance in years — buoyed by resilient risk appetite even as the U.S.-Iran conflict and an artificial-intelligence spending boom keep volatility elevated in the background.

Credo Technology leads all movers with a gain of nearly 12 percent, trailed closely by Sandisk at just over 10 percent and KLA Corp up 9.5 percent, underscoring how broadly the chip complex is participating today. Vertiv Holdings, up nearly 9 percent, extends the momentum into data-center infrastructure. Energy is the second-best sector, rising 2.15 percent, with Bloom Energy surging more than 10 percent amid renewed interest in alternative power. Industrials and Software are adding modest gains, while Materials sits flat.

On the downside, Healthcare is the session's clearest laggard, off nearly one percent as a sector, with Stryker leading declines at negative 4.8 percent. Consumer and Homebuilders are also soft. Into the close, watch whether semiconductor momentum holds as quarter-end rebalancing flows pick up — any fade in chip names or widening of Healthcare weakness could temper what has otherwise been a sharp risk-on session.

briefs.headline: Credo Technology Surges Nearly 12% to Top the Leaderboard
briefs.body: Credo Technology is the standout mover of the session, rallying 11.64 percent intraday as connectivity-chip names benefit from sustained AI infrastructure spending. The move places it well ahead of even its semiconductor peers and reflects sharpening investor conviction in high-bandwidth networking silicon heading into the second half.

briefs.headline: Bloom Energy Jumps More Than 10% as Alternative Power Gains Favor
briefs.body: Fuel-cell specialist Bloom Energy is surging 10.65 percent, helping lift the Energy sector to a 2.15 percent gain and making it the second-best performing group in mid-session trading. The move comes as the Iran conflict backdrop draws renewed attention to domestic and alternative energy sources.

briefs.headline: Wall Street Heads for Its Best Quarter in Years Despite Geopolitical Turbulence
briefs.body: Reports indicate equity markets are on pace to log their strongest quarterly advance in several years, with risk appetite holding firm even as the U.S.-Iran war and an AI-driven investment wave have generated sharp swings in global markets. Quarter-end positioning appears to be amplifying rather than dampening today's gains.

briefs.headline: Stryker Leads Healthcare Sector Lower, Off Nearly 5%
briefs.body: Stryker is the session's sharpest decliner among major names, sliding 4.8 percent and dragging the Healthcare sector to a loss of nearly one percent — the worst-performing group on the day. The weakness stands in stark contrast to the broader market's positive tone and may reflect profit-taking after recent outperformance.

briefs.headline: Egg Producers Reach $3.3 Million Settlement Over Alleged Price Inflation
briefs.body: Several large egg producers have agreed to a $3.3 million settlement resolving a government probe into alleged price inflation practices. The resolution closes a chapter on an investigation that drew scrutiny to supply-side coordination in the domestic poultry industry, though terms beyond the headline figure were not disclosed.

briefs.headline: Trump Administration Widens Higher-Borrowing Thresholds for Certain Graduate Degrees
briefs.body: The administration has expanded the list of graduate-level fields subject to elevated federal borrowing limits, a policy shift that could alter how students finance advanced education. The move is expected to increase debt capacity for specific professional programs, though critics argue it may encourage higher tuition at affected institutions.
